11: hoti hymin dedotai gnona ta mysteria tes basileias ton ouranon — to you it has been given to know the mysteries of the kingdom of the heavens — mysterion (mystery, secret) in Jewish apocalyptic usage denoted the divine plan hidden in the age but now disclosed to the elect; Daniel uses the term for what only God and his messenger can reveal (Daniel 2:47). The disciples receive the gift of understanding (synesis) that the parable-audience does not receive — not by their merit but by divine grace (dedotai, divine passive).
52: grammateus matheteutheis te basileia ton ouranon — a scribe discipled to the kingdom of the heavens — the concluding self-description of the kingdom-teacher; Matthew presents Jesus as producing a new kind of scribe who draws from both old and new. kainos kai palaios — new and old — the order is deliberate (new first): the new covenant does not discard the old treasury but brings it forward into its fulfillment; the scribe who understands the kingdom reads the old Torah through the new light Christ provides.
1: en tē hēmera ekeinē — on that day (the phrase links Matthew 13 directly to Matthew 12's rejection controversies; the parables are Jesus's response to the opposition just described) — exelthōn ho Iēsous apo tēs oikias ekathēto para tēn thalassan (Jesus going out of the house was sitting by the sea — ekathēto imperfect: was sitting, a settled posture for teaching). The movement from the house (private teaching) to the sea (public teaching) frames the parabolic discourse as a response to the situation of mixed reception described in Matthew 12.
2: kai synēchthēsan pros auton ochloi polloi — and large crowds gathered to him (synēchthēsan aorist passive of synagō: were gathered, assembled — a passive of circumstance: they gathered spontaneously) — hōste auton eis ploion embanta kathēsthai (so that he got into a boat and was sitting — embanta aorist participle: having stepped in; kathēsthai present infinitive: to be sitting): the boat provides both distance from the crowd and acoustics over water. The seated posture is the formal Jewish teaching position.
3: kai elalēsen autois polla en parabolais — and he spoke to them many things in parables (parabolais: a parable — from paraballō, to throw alongside, to compare; in Jewish usage a mashal, a saying or story that uses one domain to illuminate another) — legōn idou exēlthen ho speirōn tou speirein (saying: behold, the sower went out to sow — the Greek is literally "the one sowing went out to the sowing of him": his purpose is built into the description). The parable opens without explaining its referent; the audience must work toward the meaning.
4: en tō speirein auton — while he was sowing (en tō speirein auton: an articular infinitive with subject — the standard Matthean idiom for simultaneous action) — ha men epesen para tēn hodon (some fell along the road): the four soil types are presented in ascending order of receptivity. Para tēn hodon (alongside/along the path): the hard-packed path running through a field; seed falling there lies on the surface. Elthen ta peteina kai katephagen auta (the birds came and ate them up — kataphagō aorist: devoured completely).
5: alla de epesen epi ta petrōdē — other fell on the rocky places (petrōdē: stony ground, rocky soil — petra + -ōdēs: full of rock, rocky) — hopou ouk eichen gēn pollēn (where it did not have much soil): the problem is not the absence of soil but its shallowness. Eutheōs exanateillen (it sprang up immediately — eutheōs: the characteristic word of Mark's urgency, here in Matthew for the false urgency of shallow enthusiasm) — dia to mē echein bathos gēs (because of not having depth of soil: the articular infinitive with dia expressing cause).
6: hēliou de anateilantos ekaumatisthē — the sun rising, it was scorched (anateilantos genitive absolute: the sun having risen; ekaumatisthē aorist passive of kaumatizō: to be burned by scorching heat — the same verb in Revelation 16:8-9 for eschatological burning) — kai dia to mē echein rhizan exēranthē (and because of not having root it withered: rhizan accusative of rhiza, root; the absence of root is the explanation for both the quick sprouting and the quick dying). Root and depth are correlated: shallow soil produces rootless growth.
7: alla de epesen epi tas akanthas — other fell among the thorns (akanthai: thorns, thorn-plants; this soil has plant-growing capacity but already contains competing growth) — kai anebēsan hai akanthai kai epnixan auta (and the thorns grew up and choked them — epnixan aorist of pnigō: to choke, throttle; the same root used in 18:28 for seizing a man by the throat): the thorns did not prevent germination but prevented the seed from producing grain. The soil is good enough to grow; the competition is what kills the yield.
8: alla de epesen epi tēn gēn tēn kalēn — other fell on the good soil (gēn tēn kalēn: the good/fine soil — kalos, the word for beauty-in-goodness, appropriate quality) — kai edidou karpon (and was giving fruit — edidou imperfect: was continuing to give, an ongoing producing): the imperfect marks the sustained yield. Ho men hekaton ho de hexēkonta ho de triakonta (one a hundred, one sixty, one thirty): the yields are all above the typical ancient grain yield of 5-7x. A hundredfold is exceptional; even thirtyfold is generous. The variety is not graded bad-to-good but simply differentiated abundance.
9: ho echōn ōta akouetō — the one having ears, let them hear — the ear formula (cf. Matthew 11:15; Matthew 13:43) marks the end of the parable without explanation, creating space for active interpretation. The parable does not announce its meaning; it invites the hearer to locate themselves in the soils. Akouetō present imperative: a continuous command to hear and keep hearing. The formula also implies that not all who "hear" the parable thereby "hear" its meaning — the distinction between hearing and understanding is the theme of the next pericope.
10: dia ti en parabolais laleis autois — why do you speak to them in parables (dia ti: on account of what, why — a purpose question) — the disciples' question presupposes they have already understood the parabolic discourse differently from the crowd. Autois (to them): the distinction is between "them" (the crowd) and the disciples asking the question. The question opens the theological explanation of Matthew 13:11-17: the parables are not a simpler teaching method but a mode of disclosure that reveals to some and conceals from others.
12: hostis gar echei dothēsetai autō kai perisseuphthēsetai — for whoever has, it will be given to him and he will have abundance (dothēsetai future passive divine: it will be given by God; perisseuphthēsetai future passive: he will have it in abundance) — hostis de ouk echei kai ho echei arthēsetai ap autou (but whoever does not have, even what he has will be taken from him — arthēsetai future passive divine): the principle is not economic but revelatory: those who receive the mystery of the kingdom receive more; those who reject it lose even the initial capacity. The future passives are divine passives throughout.
13: dia touto en parabolais autois lalō hoti blepontes ou blepousin — this is why I speak to them in parables: seeing they do not see — dia touto (this is why): the parables are not despite the crowd's blindness but in response to it. Blepontes ou blepousin kai akouontes ouk akouousin oude syniousin (seeing they do not see, and hearing they do not hear nor understand — syniousin present of syniēmi, to put together, comprehend): the three-fold failure (seeing/seeing, hearing/hearing, understanding). This is a description of the crowd's spiritual state, not a permanent divine decree of blindness imposed from outside.
14: kai anaplēroutai autois hē prophēteia Ēsaiou hē legousa — and on them is being fulfilled the prophecy of Isaiah saying (anaplēroutai present passive: is being fulfilled, completed — ana + plēroō: the ana- prefix can intensify or indicate completion) — the Isaiah 6:9-10 citation follows. This is a different fulfillment formula than Matthew's usual hina plērōthē: the present tense marks ongoing fulfillment rather than a single completed event. Isaiah's commission (Matthew 6:9-10) is a description of the audience Jesus now faces.
15: epachynthē gar hē kardia tou laou toutou — for the heart of this people has grown dull/fat (epachynthē aorist passive of pachynō: to make thick/fat; the idiom for spiritual insensitivity — a heart grown so padded it cannot feel) — kai tois ōsin bareos ēkousan (and they hear with heavy/dull ears — bareos: heavily, with weight, with difficulty): the ears are overburdened, not absent. Kai tous ophthalmous autōn ekammusan (and they have closed their eyes — kammyō: to close the eyes deliberately, a voluntary shutting). The verse diagnoses willful, not merely circumstantial, blindness.
16: hymōn de makarioi hoi ophthalmoi hoti blepousin — but blessed are your eyes because they see — the Beatitude form applied to seeing and hearing, reversing the Isaiah curse. The disciples' seeing is not their achievement but a gift that marks them as blessed. Kai ta ōta hymōn hoti akouousin (and your ears because they hear): the inversion of Matthew 13:14-15 is complete — where the crowd cannot connect seeing/hearing to perceiving/understanding, the disciples' organs of perception actually function.
17: amen gar legō hymin hoti polloi prophētai kai dikaioi epethymēsan idein ha blepete — truly I tell you that many prophets and righteous people longed to see what you see (epethymēsan aorist of epithymeō: to desire ardently — the word can carry the sense of intense longing or craving; the prophets and righteous ones desired this with an unfulfilled longing) — kai ouk eidon (and did not see it). The disciples are positioned at the hinge of redemptive history: the prophets pointed forward to what is now present; the disciples receive what the prophets only anticipated.
18: hymeis oun akousate tēn parabolēn tou speirontos — you therefore hear the parable of the sower — hymeis oun (you therefore): emphatic second person, contrasting the disciples' privileged hearing with the crowd's deafness just described. Akousate aorist imperative: a sharp, focused act of hearing. The explanation that follows (Matthew 13:19-23) does not simply decode the parable but applies it — each soil type is identified by how one relates to ho logos tēs basileias (the word of the kingdom).
19: pantos akouontos ton logon tēs basileias kai mē synientos — everyone hearing the word of the kingdom and not understanding it (synientos present participle of syniēmi: not putting together, not comprehending) — erchetai ho ponēros kai harpazei to esparmenon en tē kardia autou (the evil one comes and snatches away what was sown in his heart — ho ponēros: the evil one, masculine — a personal reference to the devil as identified in Matthew 13:39; harpazei present: snatches, the same aggressive seizure-verb as 12:29 for plundering the strong man's house). The path-hearer's defining feature is non-understanding (mē synieis).
20: ho de epi ta petrōdē spareis — the one sown on the rocky places (spareis aorist passive participle of speirō: having been sown — the person is identified with the seed sown in a location) — houtos estin ho ton logon akouōn kai eutheōs meta charas lambanōn auton (this is the one who hears the word and immediately receives it with joy — eutheōs: immediately, instantly; meta charas: with joy): the immediate joyful reception is itself diagnostic. Speed and emotion without root signal shallow engagement rather than genuine reception.
21: ouk echei de rhizan en heautō — but he has no root in himself (rhizan en heautō: root within himself, not rooted in external soil only but internally without rootedness) — alla proskaireos estin (but is temporary — proskairos: for a season, time-limited): the word contains kairos (appointed time); this person is only for the appointed moment. Genomenēs de thlipseōs ē diōgmou dia ton logon eutheōs skandalizatai (when trouble or persecution comes on account of the word, immediately he stumbles/falls away — skandalizatai present passive: is caused to stumble): the same word Jesus used in 11:6 for the potential offense at himself.
22: ho de eis tas akanthas spareis — the one sown among the thorns — hē merimna tou aiōnos kai hē apatē tou ploutou sympnigei ton logon (the worry of the age and the deceitfulness of wealth choke the word — merimna tou aiōnos: the anxiety belonging to this present age; apatē tou ploutou: the deceit/deception of wealth — apatē, deceit, the word for active falsehood; wealth deceives: it promises security and satisfaction it cannot deliver). Sympnigei present: is choking — the present tense marks an ongoing process. Kai akarpos ginetai (and it becomes unfruitful — the thorn-hearer hears and perhaps even understands, but bears nothing).
23: ho de epi tēn kalēn gēn spareis — the one sown on the good soil — houtos estin ho ton logon akouōn kai synieis (this is the one who hears the word and understands it — synieis present participle of syniēmi: the understanding that is the path-hearer's absence, now present). Hos dē karpophorei kai poiei ho men hekaton ho de hexēkonta ho de triakonta (who indeed bears fruit and produces — karpophorei present: is bearing fruit; poiei present: is producing, continuously): the fruit is present-tense ongoing, and the yields (hundred, sixty, thirty) represent differentiated abundance, not a ranking of good-better-best.
24: allēn parabolēn parethēken autois legōn — he placed another parable before them (parethēken aorist of paratithēmi: to place beside, set before — a serving/presenting verb; parables are presented as food for the hearer) — hōmoiōthē hē basileia tōn ouranōn anthrōpō speironti kalon sperma en tō agrō autou (the kingdom of the heavens is like a man who sowed good seed in his field): the kingdom is compared to the whole situation, not merely to the man or the seed. The noun anthrōpō (a man) identifies the figure who is later revealed as the Son of Man (Matthew 13:37).
25: en de tō katheudein tous anthrōpous ēlthen autou ho echthros kai epespeiren zizania — but while the people were sleeping his enemy came and sowed weeds among the wheat (katheudein tous anthrōpous: the people sleeping — not negligence but normal rest; echthros: enemy; epespeiren zizania: sowed tares/weeds over — zizanion, probably Lolium temulentum, darnel, a weed that closely resembles wheat in early growth stages and is toxic) — ana meson tou sitou (among the midst of the wheat): the tares are sown specifically among, not separately from, the wheat. The mixture is intentional.
26: hote de eblastēsen ho chortos kai karpon epoiēsen — when the grain sprouted and bore fruit (eblastēsen aorist of blastanō, to sprout, germinate) — tote ephanē kai ta zizania (then the tares also appeared — ephanē aorist passive of phainō: appeared, became visible): the tares are recognizable only when both have matured enough to distinguish. Darnel is visually indistinguishable from wheat until the grain heads form; the enemy exploits this by planting them together. Early separation would destroy wheat; only at harvest is safe separation possible.
27: proselthontes de hoi douloi tou oikodespotou eipon autō — approaching, the servants of the householder said to him — kyrie ouchi kalon sperma espeiras en tō sō agrō (sir/lord, did you not sow good seed in your field? — kyrie: the address of a servant to a master): the servants presuppose the master's competence; the question expresses genuine puzzlement, not accusation. Pothen oun echei zizania (how then does it have tares? — pothen: from where? whence? a question of source): they want to identify the origin of the contamination.
28: ho de ephē autois echthros anthrōpos touto epoiēsen — he said to them: an enemy man did this (echthros anthrōpos: a hostile person — the repetition of echthros from Matthew 13:25 identifies the source without naming the devil yet; that identification comes in the interpretation of Matthew 13:39) — hoi de douloi legousin autō theleis oun apelthontes syllexōmen auta (the servants say to him: do you want us therefore, going out, to gather them up — syllexōmen aorist subjunctive: to collect, pull out): the servants' offer is premature. Their instinct for immediate purity would destroy the wheat.
29: ho de phēsin ou — he says: no (phēsin historical present: says — the vividness of direct discourse in narrative) — mēpote syllегontes ta zizania ekrizoōsēte hama autois ton siton (lest gathering the tares you uproot the wheat along with them — mēpote: lest, for fear that; ekrizoōsēte aorist subjunctive of ekrizoō: to uproot completely, to pull up by the roots): the danger is not the tares but the collateral damage of premature separation. The entanglement of root systems makes clean separation impossible until harvest.
30: aphete synauxanesthai amphotera heōs tou therismou — let both grow together until the harvest (synauxanesthai present passive infinitive: to grow together, co-grow — the syn- prefix marks simultaneous growth) — kai en kairō tou therismou erō tois theristais (and at the time of the harvest I will say to the harvesters — kairō: the appointed moment): the harvesters are identified as angels in Matthew 13:39. The harvest-sequence (weeds first → bundled → burned; wheat → gathered into the barn) is the reverse of typical priorities: removal of bad before collection of good, emphasizing the thoroughness of the final separation.
31: allēn parabolēn parethēken autois legōn — he placed another parable before them — homoia estin hē basileia tōn ouranōn kokkō sinapeōs (the kingdom of the heavens is like a grain of mustard seed — kokkos sinapeōs: a mustard grain, probably Sinapis alba or Brassica nigra): the kingdom is compared to the whole scenario, not just the seed. Hon labōn anthrōpos espeiren en tō agrō autou (which a man took and sowed in his field — labōn...espeiren: the two-verb sequence of human action, deliberate planting).
32: ho mikroteron men estin pantōn tōn spermatōn — which is smaller than all seeds (mikroteron comparative: smaller — the mustard seed was proverbially the smallest in Palestinian agricultural knowledge; pantōn tōn spermatōn: of all seeds, in the field/garden context; botanically not the world's smallest seed) — hotan de auxēthē meizon tōn lachanōn estin kai ginetai dendron (but when it grows it is greater than the garden plants and becomes a tree — dendron: a tree, though mustard shrubs are not true trees): the contrast between the proverbially small beginning and the bird-sheltering result is the point, not botanical precision. Ta peteina tou ouranou...kataskhēnoun — birds of the sky nesting in its branches echoes Ezekiel 17:22-24; Matthew 31:6; Daniel 4:12 (the nations sheltering under the great kingdom-tree).
33: allēn parabolēn elalēsen autois — he spoke another parable to them — homoia estin hē basileia tōn ouranōn zymē (the kingdom of the heavens is like leaven/yeast — zymē: leavening agent, normally a negative symbol in Jewish thought for corrupting influence, e.g., Passover unleavened bread requirements): the use of leaven as a positive image is striking. Hēn labousa gynē (which a woman took — the woman is the agent; the three sata = three sata measures, about 13 liters each = ~39 liters of flour total: an enormous quantity, enough for a large communal meal). Enekrypsen eis aleurou sata tria (she hid in three measures of flour — enekrypsen: hid, embedded — the leaven is not mixed on the surface but hidden within). Heōs hou ezymōthē holon — until all of it was leavened: the totality, not just the portion.
34: tauta panta elalēsen ho Iēsous en parabolais tois ochlois — all these things Jesus spoke in parables to the crowds (en parabolais: in parables — not occasionally but as the exclusive mode for crowd teaching) — kai chōris parabolēs ouden elalei autois (and without a parable he said nothing to them): the absolute statement covers this discourse; Matthew does not claim Jesus never taught the crowds directly anywhere, but that in this parabolic discourse, parable was the only vehicle. The verse prepares the fulfillment citation of Matthew 13:35.
35: hopōs plērōthē to rēthen dia tou prophētou legontos — in order that what was spoken through the prophet might be fulfilled — the citation is Psalms 78:2 (Asaph): anoixō en parabolais to stoma mou (I will open my mouth in parables). Ereugomai kekrymena apo katabolēs kosmou (I will utter/pour out things hidden since the foundation of the world — ereugomai future of ereugomai: to belch/utter forth forcefully — a strong emission verb, used in LXX Psalms 19:2 for the heavens pouring out speech). Katabolēs kosmou (foundation/laying down of the world): the hidden things predate creation.
36: tote apheis tous ochlous ēlthen eis tēn oikian — then leaving the crowds he came into the house — the movement from public (boat/crowds) to private (house/disciples) mirrors 13:1 in reverse. Kai prosēlthon autō hoi mathētai autou legontes diasaphēson hēmin tēn parabolēn tōn zizaniōn (and his disciples came to him saying: explain to us the parable of the weeds — diasaphēson aorist imperative of diasapheō: to make thoroughly clear, explain in detail — a stronger verb than simply "tell us the meaning"): the disciples, inside the house with Jesus, ask for the full disclosure that the crowd cannot receive.
37: ho speirōn to kalon sperma estin ho huios tou anthrōpou — the one sowing the good seed is the Son of Man — the explanation is an allegory: each element is decoded. Ho de agros estin ho kosmos (the field is the world — kosmos: the whole created order, not just Israel): the kingdom's scope is universal, not national. The Son of Man is identified as the sower, identifying Jesus's mission with the planting of the good-seed people in the world-field. The identification of agros = kosmos is exegetically significant: the field is not the church but the world.
38: to de kalon sperma houtoi eisin hoi huioi tēs basileias — the good seed are the sons of the kingdom (huioi tēs basileias: a Semitic genitive-of-characterization: those who belong to the kingdom, those shaped by it) — ta de zizania houtoi eisin hoi huioi tou ponērou (the tares are the sons of the evil one — hoi huioi tou ponērou: sons of the evil one, parallel to "sons of the kingdom"; those shaped by and belonging to the evil one): the two peoples coexist in the world, not in the church. The parable is about eschatological separation of humanity, not church discipline.
39: ho de echthros ho speiras auta estin ho diabolos — the enemy who sowed them is the devil (diabolos: the slanderer, accuser — the Greek translation of Hebrew satan) — ho de therismos synteia aiōnos estin (the harvest is the end of the age — synteleia aiōnos: the completion/consummation of the age): synteleia is the word for final consummation, not merely cessation; the age comes to its intended completion. Hoi de theristai angeloi eisin (the harvesters are angels): the agents of final separation are divine, not human — the disciples are not to perform this separation themselves.
40: hōsper oun syllegetai ta zizania kai pyri katakaieta — as therefore the tares are gathered and burned in fire (katakaieta present passive: are being burned up completely) — houtōs estai en tē synteleia tou aiōnos (so it will be at the end of the age): the harvest-scenario is the paradigm for the final judgment. The fire is not punitive inflammation added from outside but the natural destiny of material fit only for burning. Synteleia tou aiōnos is repeated from Matthew 13:39, marking this as the controlling temporal reference for the entire parable's application.
41: apostelei ho huios tou anthrōpou tous angelous autou — the Son of Man will send his angels (apostelei future: will send with a commission) — kai syllexousin ek tēs basileias autou panta ta skandala kai tous poiountas tēn anomian (and they will gather out of his kingdom everything that causes stumbling and those practicing lawlessness — panta ta skandala: all the causes-of-stumbling, the trap-springs; tous poiountas tēn anomian: those actively practicing lawlessness — anomia: the absence of law, the refusal of God's order; cf. 7:23 where the same phrase closes the Sermon on the Mount's false-prophet warning).
42: kai balousin autous eis tēn kaminon tou pyros — and they will throw them into the furnace of fire (kaminon tou pyros: the furnace of fire — kaminos is the word for a kiln or smelting furnace, an intense enclosed fire; appearing also in Revelation 1:15; Matthew 9:2) — ekei estai ho klauthmos kai ho brygmos tōn odontōn (there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth — klauthmos: loud weeping, lamentation; brygmos tōn odontōn: grinding/gnashing of teeth — a phrase Matthew uses six times, always in eschatological contexts): the formula describes the combination of grief (klauthmos) and rage/anguish (brygmos) in final separation.
43: tote hoi dikaioi eklamphousin hōs ho hēlios — then the righteous will shine like the sun (eklamphousin future of eklampō: to shine out, to blaze forth — the ek- prefix marks emergence into full radiance; the verb is only used here in the NT) — en tē basileia tou patros autōn (in the kingdom of their Father): the righteous inherit the Father's kingdom as children, not merely subjects. Ho echōn ōta akouetō — the one having ears, let them hear: the ear formula closes the parable-interpretation, marking the eschatological disclosure as requiring the same active hearing as the parables themselves.
44: homoia estin hē basileia tōn ouranōn thēsaurō kekrymmenō en tō agrō — the kingdom of the heavens is like treasure hidden in a field (thēsauros: treasure, a stored collection of value; kekrymmenō perfect passive participle: having been hidden, currently hidden) — hon heurōn anthrōpos ekrypsen (which a man found and hid again — he re-conceals the treasure to secure his claim before purchase): the re-hiding is not deceptive but protective. Kai apo tēs charas autou hypagei kai pōlei panta hosa echei — and from his joy he goes and sells all he has (apo tēs charas: from/out of his joy — the joy is the motivation for the total sell-off; the kingdom is recognized as worth everything by one who encounters it).
45: palin homoia estin hē basileia tōn ouranōn emporos zētounti kalous margaritas — again the kingdom of the heavens is like a merchant seeking fine pearls (emporos: a traveling merchant, a wholesale trader — a person who knows the pearl market; kalous margaritas: fine/beautiful pearls — kalos, fine in quality, not just visually attractive): the merchant is an expert in pearls, not a casual buyer. His expertise is what enables him to recognize the exceptional pearl when he finds it.
46: heurōn de hena polytimō margaritēn — having found one extremely valuable pearl (polytimō: of great price, highly valued — poly + timē: much-price, maximum value) — apelthōn pepraken panta hosa eichen kai ēgorasen auton (going away he sold all that he had and bought it): the merchant's expertise culminates in recognition and total investment. Unlike the hidden-treasure man who stumbled on the find, the merchant was actively seeking. Both parables end with total divestment for total acquisition: the kingdom worth everything is actually acquired by those who count it as everything.
47: palin homoia estin hē basileia tōn ouranōn sagēnē blētheisē eis tēn thalassan — again the kingdom of the heavens is like a dragnet thrown into the sea (sagēnē: a large dragnet or seine net — a net weighted at the bottom and with floats at the top, dragged through the water and sweeping up everything in its path, not selective) — kai ek pantos genous synagagousē (and gathering fish of every kind — ek pantos genous: from every kind; synagagousē aorist participle of synagō: having gathered): the net is not a trap for a specific species but a total sweep. The sorting happens after the catch, not during.
48: hēn hote eplērōthē anabibazein epi ton aigialon — when it was full they drew it up onto the shore (eplērōthē aorist passive: was filled, reached capacity; anabibazein: to drag up) — kai kathisantes synelaexan ta kala eis angē (and sitting down they gathered the good into containers — kathisantes aorist participle: having sat down, the formal working posture; angeia: containers, vessels): the parable parallels the weeds-and-wheat explanation. The good fish are gathered first (vessels), then the bad are cast out — the inverse order from Matthew 13:30, but the same outcome of final separation. The ta kala (the good/beautiful) contrasts with ta sapra (the rotten, Matthew 13:48b).
49: houtōs estai en tē synteleia tou aiōnos — so it will be at the end of the age — the repetition of the synteleia formula (cf. Matthew 13:40) frames the net-parable as the same eschatological scenario. Exeleusontai hoi angeloi kai aphoriousin tous ponērous ek mesou tōn dikaiōn (the angels will go out and separate the wicked from the midst of the righteous — aphoriousin future of aphorizō: to mark off by boundaries, to separate distinctly): the angels separate the wicked from the midst of the righteous — the mixture is inside the good, as in the field.
50: kai balousin autous eis tēn kaminon tou pyros — and they will throw them into the furnace of fire — the formula repeats Matthew 13:42 verbatim: ekei estai ho klauthmos kai ho brygmos tōn odontōn (there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth). The repetition of the eschatological formula in both parable-interpretations (weeds and net) emphasizes the unity of the teaching: the same final separation is described from two angles (the field/humanity as a whole; the sea/the full scope of the mission's catch).
51: synēkate tauta panta — have you understood all these things? (synēkate aorist of syniēmi: the understanding-verb used throughout Matthew 13, Matthew 13:13,14,15,19,23; to bring together, comprehend) — legousin autō nai (they say to him: yes): the disciples' affirmative answer to Jesus's direct question sets up the final parable of Matthew 13:52. Their understanding (however partial) qualifies them as the "scribes instructed in the kingdom of the heavens" described next. The question and answer mark the transition from parable-hearing to parable-understanding.
53: kai egeneto hote etelesen ho Iēsous tas parabolēs tautas — and it happened when Jesus had finished these parables (etelesen aorist of teleō: to complete — the discourse-closing formula, cf. Matthew 7:28; Matthew 11:1) — metēren ekeithen (he departed from there): the geographical transition marks the end of the parabolic discourse and the beginning of the hometown rejection pericope. The parables that revealed the kingdom to disciples and concealed it from the unperceptive now face a concrete test: the response of Jesus's own hometown.
54: kai elthōn eis tēn patrida autou edidasken autous en tē synagōgē autōn — coming to his hometown he was teaching them in their synagogue (patrida: homeland, native country — hometown; autōn: their synagogue, the possessive marking the same institutional distance as Matthew 12:9) — hōste ekplēssesthai autous (so that they were astonished — ekplēssesthai imperfect passive: were being knocked out, struck with astonishment — the strongest word for amazement): the reaction is not neutral wonder but surprised displacement. Pothen toutō hē sophia autē kai hai dynameis (from where does this man get this wisdom and these powers? — pothen: whence, the question of source that reveals they have no framework for the answer).
55: ouch houtos estin ho tou tektonos huios — isn't this the son of the craftsman? (tektōn: craftsman, worker in materials — the word covers carpenter, builder, stonemason, woodworker; it is not narrowly "carpenter" in the modern sense but a skilled craftsperson in the construction trades; Mark 6:3 applies the same term directly to Jesus) — Mariam: Mary is named directly; Iakōbos, Iōsēph, Simōn, Ioudas: the four brothers named. The family's ordinariness is the problem: the crowd's knowledge of Jesus's earthly origins prevents them from receiving his heavenly origin.
56: kai hai adelphai autou ouchai pasai pros hēmas eisin — aren't all his sisters here with us (hai adelphai: the sisters are mentioned but unnamed; their number is implied by pasai, all — suggesting at least two, probably more) — pothen oun toutō tauta panta (where then does this man get all these things? — the repeated pothen question frames the hometown rejection as a failure to reconcile Jesus's known origins with his unprecedented claims and deeds): the neighbors' intimate knowledge of his family becomes a barrier rather than a credential.
57: kai eskandalizonto en autō — and they were being caused to stumble by him (eskandalizonto imperfect passive: were being continually tripped up — the passive marks the cause as Jesus himself; skandalizō: to set a trap, to cause to stumble, the same word used for the path-hearer in 13:21 and for the stumbling-block of Matthew 11:6) — ho de Iēsous eipen autois: Jesus's response cites a proverb: ouk estin prophētēs atimos ei mē en tē patridi kai en tē oikia autou (a prophet is not dishonored except in his hometown and in his own house — atimos: without honor, dishonored): the proverb is attested in varied forms in ancient literature but receives its most pointed application here.
58: kai ouk epoiēsen ekei dynameis pollas dia tēn apistian autōn — and he did not do many miracles there because of their unbelief (apistia: unbelief, lack of faith — a- privative + pistis: the absence of the faith that is the receptor for miraculous healing; cf. Matthew 9:29: "according to your faith let it be done") — Matthew says pollas (many) rather than Mark's "none"; some healing occurred but the scale was drastically limited. The verse is not a theological claim that Jesus lacked power but that the reception of miracle requires the soil of faith; in its absence, miracles remain largely unsown.
